critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

This paper reports a reduction in ICU transfers among flagged patients (18% to 11%) after launching a sepsis early-warning model and claims this is evidence that the model improved early sepsis recognition and downstream care. The causal inference is not supported.

The primary problem is a concurrent confound: in the same launch quarter, Hospital A adopted a nurse-driven sepsis bundle that allowed antibiotics and lactate testing before physician authorization, and also increased evening staffing. These are direct care-process interventions that independently affect sepsis outcomes and ICU transfer rates. A faster treatment pathway under the nurse-driven bundle alone would be expected to reduce ICU transfers regardless of any AI alerting system.

The study has no contemporaneous control. Hospital B was excluded due to EHR timestamp noise — a data quality problem that could have been addressed through cleaning or imputation. Without a control hospital experiencing similar secular trends, the pre/post comparison at a single site cannot separate model effects from bundle effects from natural trends.

The model's retrospective AUROC on the prior year's data demonstrates it can rank sepsis risk. This is a separate claim from demonstrating that deploying the model changed outcomes — the former is predictive validity, the latter is causal effect estimation, and they require different study designs.

High clinician agreement with top-decile alerts and a fixed deployment threshold are operational strengths that suggest the model is producing credible risk scores. These do not resolve the causal confound.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — the model may have genuine risk-stratification value, but the causal outcome claim requires a contemporaneous control or staggered rollout analysis to establish.
